_comment: Seasonal schedules for Garden Ops — Edmonton zone 4a. Derived from web research (2026-06-14) + the existing registry. Holds the data the registry lacks: harvest windows, spring-prep and fall/winter-prep tasks, plus a garden-wide month-by-month task calendar. The dashboard build script reads this alongside plants.json/zones.json. Source-of-truth rule: plants.json stays authoritative for watering/fertilizer/pests/overwinter; this file ADDS harvest + dated tasks. Dates are MM-DD (local, America/Edmonton). 'year1' flags plants planted 2026 that are establishing — little or no harvest expected this season.

harvest.signal: Lift when the lower 3–4 leaves have browned but upper leaves are still green (each green leaf = one bulb wrapper). Don't wait for the whole top to die.
harvest.post_harvest: Cure whole plants 2–3 weeks in a dry, airy, shaded spot, then trim. Save the biggest bulbs to replant Oct 1–15.
harvest.pre_harvest: Snap scapes ~late June (one curl) and eat them; stop watering ~2 weeks before lifting so bulbs cure in dry ground.

spring_prep.task: Garlic shoots push through mulch — pull excess mulch back to bare soil; side-dress with nitrogen ONCE in early spring (last feed of the year).

fall_winter_prep.task: Replant saved cloves 10–15 cm deep, 15 cm apart, pointy end up; mulch after the ground starts to freeze.

harvest.window: New: late Jul · Main: mid-Sep – early Oct
harvest.signal: New potatoes ~2–3 weeks after flowering (steal a few). Main crop: wait ~2 weeks AFTER the tops die back so skins set. A light Sept frost on the tops is fine — tubers are safe underground.
harvest.post_harvest: Cure in the dark 10–14 days at cool temp / high humidity to thicken skins, then store cool and dark. Never store greened (solanine) tubers.
harvest.pre_harvest: Keep soil evenly moist through flowering (tuber set); hill stems through July so no tubers green.

fall_winter_prep.task: After harvest, clear all vines and stray tubers from B4 (volunteers harbour disease). Bed is bare for winter.

harvest.signal: Pick outer leaves once they're hand-sized, leaving the central crown to keep producing. Frost makes kale SWEETER — keep harvesting after frosts.
harvest.post_harvest: Under the B5 hoop tunnel you can cut into November. Done when buried in snow.

fall_winter_prep.task: Close/seal the hoop tunnel ends on frost nights to push the kale + salad harvest deep into fall.

harvest.signal: Cut leaves at ~10 cm, leave the growing point — it regrows. Arugula & cilantro bolt in July heat (harvest hard, then re-sow).
harvest.post_harvest: Under the hoop, harvest into late fall.
harvest.pre_harvest: Succession-sow a new patch every ~3 weeks for continuous greens.

spring_prep.task: Direct-sow the first salad/arugula/cilantro/lettuce under the hoop — cold-tolerant, goes in before tender crops.

harvest.signal: Romas ripen fully red on the vine Aug–Sep. HARD DEADLINE: strip every fruit, including green, before the first fall frost.
harvest.post_harvest: Ripen green tomatoes indoors in a paper bag (a ripe apple speeds it). Pull dead plants after harvest.
harvest.pre_harvest: Even watering prevents blossom-end rot on Romas. Pinch the growing tip ~Aug 20 so the plant ripens what it has.

fall_winter_prep.task: Annual — harvest all, then compost healthy vines / bag diseased ones. Clear B3.

harvest.signal: Pick as fruit colours up Aug–Sep; strip everything, green included, before the first fall frost.
harvest.post_harvest: Ripen green fruit indoors in a paper bag.
harvest.pre_harvest: Cage/stake early; top the plant ~Aug 20 to push ripening.

fall_winter_prep.task: Annual — harvest all and clear the plants from B3.

harvest.signal: Pick green/coloured as wanted from mid-Aug. Peppers are damaged even at +2 °C — get EVERYTHING in before any frost, no grace period.
harvest.post_harvest: Green peppers will finish ripening to red indoors on a windowsill.
harvest.pre_harvest: Steady moisture + calcium to avoid blossom-end rot.

fall_winter_prep.task: Annual — strip all fruit the day a frost is forecast, then clear B2.

harvest.signal: If zucchini/summer squash: pick young at 15–20 cm, every 1–2 days — frequent picking keeps it producing. If a winter type: harvest before frost when the rind is hard.
harvest.post_harvest: Summer squash: use fresh, doesn't store. Winter squash: cure in warmth ~10 days then store cool.
harvest.pre_harvest: Water at the soil line, not the leaves, to limit powdery mildew.

fall_winter_prep.task: Annual — harvest, pull the vine, clear B3.

harvest.signal: Pinch from the top above a leaf pair to keep it bushy; pinch out all flower buds. Most frost-tender plant in the garden — sulks below 10 °C, damaged below ~4 °C.
harvest.post_harvest: Do a hard harvest (pesto / freeze) before the first cold nights, or pot one plant up and bring it indoors in early Sept.
harvest.pre_harvest: It is the headline of every frost alert — cover or harvest on any night forecast below ~7 °C.

fall_winter_prep.task: Annual — harvest hard or bring one potted plant inside before the first cold snap.

harvest.signal: Snip leaves anytime. After it flowers, shear the whole clump to 5 cm for a flush of fresh tender regrowth.
harvest.post_harvest: Bulletproof zone-3 perennial — comes back every year.

spring_prep.task: First fresh chives of the year emerge — top-dress with compost, no other feeding needed.

fall_winter_prep.task: Fully hardy — nothing to do. Dies back, returns in spring.

harvest.window: late Jun – Jul (+ Sep flush if everbearing)
harvest.signal: June-bearing: one big 2–3 week crop late June–July. Everbearing/day-neutral: June crop + a second flush in September. Pick fully red, every 1–2 days; net against birds.
harvest.post_harvest: June-bearing: feed a balanced fertilizer right AFTER the crop finishes. Pinch runners to keep energy in the fruiting crowns.
harvest.pre_harvest: Steady moisture during fruiting; straw/mulch under berries keeps them clean and slug-free.

spring_prep.task: Pull winter straw off the crowns once snow is gone and growth starts; check for new green. Re-lay a thin clean mulch in early May to keep fruit off soil.

fall_winter_prep.task: Cover crowns with 5–10 cm clean straw AFTER the ground freezes; pull it back in April.

harvest.signal: Floricane type — fruits on canes that overwintered. THIS year's new canes fruit NEXT year, so 2026 yield is light/none. Berries are ripe when dull-black and pull free easily.
harvest.post_harvest: Whether you get fruit depends entirely on winter cane survival — see the burial task below.

spring_prep.task: Uncover the buried canes once hard frost risk eases, lift and tie them back to the support, prune any winter-killed (brown, brittle) wood to live tissue.

fall_winter_prep.window: late Oct (after a few hard frosts)
fall_winter_prep.task: Bend canes to the ground, pin, and bury under 15–20 cm of leaves/mulch (snow on top is ideal). Cane survival = next year's harvest.

harvest.signal: Most tender bramble here (zone 5–6). Floricane type — light/no fruit in 2026; berries ripe when deep maroon-black and soft.
harvest.post_harvest: Harvest hinges on near-perfect cane burial — exposed canes die around −20 °C.
harvest.pre_harvest: Watch for chlorosis (yellow leaf / green veins) — alkaline soil; treat with chelated iron.

spring_prep.task: Uncover and retie canes like the blackberry; prune winter-killed wood.

fall_winter_prep.task: Bury canes completely — MORE critical than the blackberry. Add an extra straw layer.

harvest.signal: First season — establishing, no harvest expected. A mature sour/Evans-type cherry ripens dark red late July–August; net against birds.
harvest.post_harvest: Variety is unconfirmed — sour/Romance-series survive Edmonton; a sweet cherry likely won't.
harvest.pre_harvest: Deep weekly soak this first season to establish roots.

spring_prep.task: Inspect for black-knot galls and winter dieback; prune out any black knots 15–20 cm below the gall and burn the prunings.

fall_winter_prep.task: Deep-water before freeze-up; mulch the root zone AFTER the ground freezes; put a rodent guard on the trunk. Add burlap if it proves to be a sweet cherry.

harvest.window: late Sep (small main crop, if any)
harvest.signal: Rebuilding from winter dieback this year. Olympian fruits on NEW wood (main crop) — a short Edmonton season ripens few, if any, in a recovery year. Ripe figs droop on the stem and soften.
harvest.post_harvest: Long term, expect a modest main crop in warm years once the plant has size.
harvest.pre_harvest: Don't let potted figs dry out — drought stress drops fruit.

spring_prep.task: Bring pots out of the garage after hard frosts pass; place in shade first, then add sun over 7–10 days to avoid sunscald. Spring pruning is fine (fruits on new wood).

fall_winter_prep.task: Let a light frost trigger leaf drop/dormancy, then move pots to the garage at 0–7 °C; water barely-moist ~monthly. NEVER plant in ground in Edmonton.

harvest.signal: Establishing in its acid pot this year. Mature highbush ripens late July–Aug; pick when fully blue and they roll off into your hand. Net against birds.
harvest.post_harvest: Keep it potted forever — Edmonton ground pH 7.5–8 will kill it.
harvest.pre_harvest: Use rainwater (tap water is alkaline). Yellow leaf / green veins = pH creeping up → add sulphur / chelated iron.

spring_prep.task: Bring the pot out of winter storage after hard frost; top up acidic peat mix; first acid feed.

fall_winter_prep.task: Overwinter the POT in an unheated garage (or buried/heavily mulched against the house). It needs winter chill — do not keep warm.

harvest.signal: Establishing — no harvest year 1. Hardy prairie grapes (Valiant/Frontenac) ripen Sep–Oct; taste-test for sweetness, don't go by colour alone.
harvest.post_harvest: Variety decides everything: Valiant/Beta/Frontenac can go in-ground & cane-bury; a table grape stays potted & tender.
harvest.pre_harvest: Go easy on feeding — overfeeding makes vine, not fruit.

spring_prep.task: Bring pot out after hard frost; prune while still dormant (grapes bleed if pruned late).

fall_winter_prep.task: Dormant pot into the garage like the figs until the variety/hardiness is confirmed.

harvest.signal: Unidentified resprouts (goji? haskap?). If haskap: ripe late June–early July (one of the earliest berries). If goji: ripens late summer–fall. ID before counting on a harvest.
harvest.post_harvest: Both goji (z3) and haskap (z2) are bombproof here — no winter protection needed once ID'd.
harvest.pre_harvest: Prune the dead winter tops back to live wood; send a leaf close-up for ID when growth fills in.

spring_prep.task: Prune dead tops to the live chartreuse regrowth; firm soil over any exposed roots.

fall_winter_prep.task: If goji or haskap, fully hardy — leave it. Confirm ID first.

harvest.window: TBD — resolve crop vs weed first
harvest.signal: Photo review verdict: compost weed flush (smartweed/lady's-thumb-like), not a sown crop. No harvest.
harvest.pre_harvest: Shallow-hoe the bed now while seedlings are tiny; spare any scattered beet/chard or dill-like crop seedlings.

calendar.title: Uncover overwintered plants
calendar.detail: Pull winter straw off strawberry crowns and back from perennials once snow is gone. Unbury & retie bramble canes (blackberry, boysenberry). Prune winter-killed wood to live tissue. Check cherry for black knot.

calendar.title: Garlic emerges — last feed
calendar.detail: Garlic shoots push through the mulch. Pull excess mulch to bare soil and give the ONE nitrogen feed of its year (none after, or bulbs stay small).

calendar.title: Bring potted tender plants out of the garage
calendar.detail: After hard frosts ease, move figs, blueberry and grape out of winter storage. Shade first, then add sun over 7–10 days to prevent sunscald. Prune grape & fig while still dormant.

calendar.title: Soil prep & cool-crop sowing
calendar.detail: Work compost into beds once soil hits ~10 °C. Direct-sow salad greens, arugula, cilantro, lettuce under the B5 hoop. Plant potatoes early–mid May.

calendar.title: Frost cloth on standby
calendar.detail: Average last frost is May 7 but frost can strike into early June. Keep frost cloth ready for tender transplants; the safe tender-planting date is the May long weekend (~May 18).

calendar.title: First feed of perennials & shrubs
calendar.detail: As growth starts, give strawberries, brambles and (year 2+) cherry their balanced spring feed. Start figs on half-strength 10-10-10 and blueberry on ACID fertilizer only.

calendar.title: Snap garlic scapes
calendar.detail: Cut/snap scapes after one curl (before they straighten) so energy goes to the bulb — leaving them can cut bulb size ~30%. Eat them (garlic-scape pesto).

calendar.title: Bramble mid-June feed (last one)
calendar.detail: Light second feed for blackberry & boysenberry. HARD STOP all bramble nitrogen by July 31.

calendar.title: Strawberry harvest
calendar.detail: Main crop late June–July. Pick fully red every 1–2 days, net against birds. Feed June-bearing types right after the crop finishes; pinch runners.

calendar.title: Stop watering garlic
calendar.detail: About 2 weeks before lifting, stop watering B5 garlic so the bulbs cure in dry ground.

calendar.title: Harvest garlic
calendar.detail: Lift when the lower 3–4 leaves brown but the top stays green. Cure whole plants 2–3 weeks in shade; save the biggest bulbs for October replanting.

calendar.title: Fruit-set feed for fruiting veg
calendar.detail: Switch tomatoes, peppers and squash to a bloom/fruit fertilizer (5-10-10) at first fruit set; add calcium for peppers/tomatoes to prevent blossom-end rot.

calendar.title: Summer squash / zucchini harvest
calendar.detail: Pick young (15–20 cm) every 1–2 days to keep production going.

calendar.title: STOP nitrogen on perennials, shrubs & trees
calendar.detail: Hard cutoff — late feeding pushes soft growth that winter-kills. Applies to brambles, strawberries, cherry, figs, blueberry, grape. Vegetables can keep feeding.

calendar.title: Tomato & pepper harvest
calendar.detail: Pick as fruit colours up. Top tomato plants ~Aug 20 to push ripening. Strip EVERYTHING (green included) before the first fall frost.

calendar.title: Overwintering planning window opens
calendar.detail: Run the overwintering skill for the per-plant dig-up / bring-in / cover / mulch checklist. Line up garage space for the pots and straw for the beds.

calendar.title: Hard-harvest basil before cold nights
calendar.detail: Basil sulks below 10 °C and is damaged below ~4 °C. Make pesto / freeze it, or pot one plant up and bring it indoors when nights drop below ~7 °C.

calendar.title: First-frost watch — cover to extend season
calendar.detail: First fall frost averages Sept 22 but can hit early September. One early frost is usually followed by 2–3 more good weeks — covering tender crops that one night buys real harvest. Basil/tomato/pepper/squash are the priorities.

calendar.title: Harvest & cure potatoes
calendar.detail: Wait ~2 weeks after the tops die back, then dig. Cure in the dark 10–14 days (cool, humid) to set skins; store cool & dark. Light frost on the tops is fine.

calendar.title: Run the hoop tunnel for fall greens
calendar.detail: Seal the B5 hoop ends on frost nights to carry kale + salad into November. Frost sweetens the kale.

calendar.title: Replant garlic
calendar.detail: Plant the saved biggest cloves 10–15 cm deep, 15 cm apart, pointy end up. Mulch once the ground starts to freeze.

calendar.title: Move tender pots to the garage
calendar.detail: Let a light frost trigger fig leaf-drop, then move figs, blueberry and grape pots to the unheated garage (0–7 °C). Water barely-moist ~monthly. They need cold — do not keep warm.

calendar.title: Bury bramble canes
calendar.detail: After a few hard frosts, bend canes down, pin, and bury under 15–20 cm of leaves/mulch (snow on top is ideal). Boysenberry needs an extra straw layer. Cane survival = next year's berries.

calendar.title: Deep water before freeze-up
calendar.detail: Give the cherry and any shrubs a deep late-October soak before the ground freezes. Blow out drip lines (B3/B4/B5) so they don't split.

calendar.title: Mulch AFTER the ground freezes
calendar.detail: Once the ground is frozen, lay 5–10 cm clean straw over strawberry crowns and mulch the cherry root zone + rodent guard. Mulching after freeze keeps the ground frozen through chinooks (freeze-thaw is the killer). Snow cover is the best insulator.

calendar.title: Last kale & greens under the hoop
calendar.detail: Keep harvesting kale and hardy greens from the hoop tunnel until they're buried in snow.
